title: Gelijke rechten, maar geen gelijke pensioenen: de gender gap in Nederlandse tweedepijlerpensioenen (Design Paper 178)
key_stats: Gepensioneerde vrouwen ontvangen gemiddeld ruim 40% minder pensioen dan mannen (NL)
Nederland behoort internationaal tot de landen met de grootste pensioenkloof
Geen eenvoudige juridische oplossing — de bal ligt bij regelingontwerp, werkgevers en communicatie
excerpt: Gepensioneerde vrouwen in Nederland ontvangen gemiddeld ruim 40% minder pensioen dan mannen, ondanks formeel gelijke pensioenrechten. Dit Netspar Design Paper van onderzoekers van de Universiteit Leiden (Kali, Been, Knoef en Van Marwijk Kooy) ontleedt de oorzaken van de kloof in tweedepijlerpensioenen: deeltijdwerk, loonverschillen en minder genereuze regelingen in sectoren waar veel vrouwen werken. Nederland behoort internationaal tot de landen met de grootste pensioenkloof. De auteurs concluderen dat er geen eenvoudige juridische oplossing is, wat de bal nadrukkelijk bij regelingontwerp, werkgevers en communicatie legt. Het wetenschappelijke standaardwerk onder de Nederlandse pensioenkloof-discussie.

title: Pensioenkloof tussen mannen en vrouwen enorm
key_stats: Vrouwen ontvangen gemiddeld 40% minder pensioen dan mannen
Honderden euro's per maand minder te besteden na pensionering
Slechts de helft van de Nederlanders kent de pensioenkloof
5 op de 10 vrouwen maakt zich grote zorgen over de kloof, tegenover 3 op de 10 mannen
excerpt: Onderzoek gepresenteerd door ABP en Wijzer in geldzaken tijdens de Pensioen3daagse 2024: vrouwen ontvangen gemiddeld 40% minder pensioen dan mannen en hebben na pensionering honderden euro's per maand minder te besteden. Slechts de helft van de Nederlanders kent de pensioenkloof; 5 op de 10 vrouwen maakt zich er grote zorgen over, tegenover 3 op de 10 mannen. Belangrijkste oorzaken: deeltijdwerk, de loonkloof en onbetaalde zorgtaken. Voor pensioenuitvoerders relevant omdat levensgebeurtenissen zoals scheiding of overlijden van de partner de kloof acuut maken — een direct aanknopingspunt voor gerichte communicatie en keuzebegeleiding.

title: Minder 60-plussers onder AOW-leeftijd al met pensioen
key_stats: 27% van de vrouwen heeft geen aanvullende pensioenopbouw via een werkgever, tegenover 18% van de mannen
Aanvullende pensioenaanspraak boven €20.887 per jaar: 44,1% van de mannen vs 18,1% van de vrouwen
In alle onderzochte groepen bouwen vrouwen vaker geen of minder aanvullend pensioen op
excerpt: CBS-cijfers (februari 2026) over pensioenopbouw van 60-plussers onder de AOW-leeftijd in 2024. Vrouwen bouwen structureel minder aanvullend pensioen op: 27% van de vrouwen heeft geen aanvullende pensioenopbouw via een werkgever, tegenover 18% van de mannen. Onder werknemers heeft 44,1% van de mannen een aanvullende pensioenaanspraak boven €20.887 per jaar, tegenover slechts 18,1% van de vrouwen. In alle onderzochte groepen bouwen vrouwen vaker geen of minder aanvullend pensioen op. De meest actuele officiële statistiek om de Nederlandse pensioenkloof te kwantificeren en doelgroepkeuzes te onderbouwen.

title: Rapport Pensioenpeiling 2024
key_stats: Peiling onder 1.541 werkenden van 25 tot 67 jaar
Vrouwen behoren tot de groepen die het minst over hun pensioen weten
Nibud: intensiveer de voorlichting juist op deze groepen
excerpt: Nibud peilde onder 1.541 werkenden van 25 tot 67 jaar de kennis van het nieuwe pensioenstelsel. De bekendheid met de veranderingen is groter onder oudere werkenden, theoretisch opgeleiden en mannen; vrouwen behoren samen met jongvolwassenen en praktisch opgeleiden tot de groepen die het minst over hun pensioen weten. Nibud adviseert de voorlichting juist op deze groepen te intensiveren. Voor pensioenuitvoerders onderstreept dit dat communicatie en keuzebegeleiding rond de Wet toekomst pensioenen gender-bewust ontworpen moeten worden om vrouwelijke deelnemers daadwerkelijk te bereiken.

title: The 2024 Pension Adequacy Report: current and future income adequacy in old age in the EU
key_stats: Women in the EU receive on average 26.1% lower pension income than men
5.3% of EU women receive no pension at all
Country profiles for all 27 member states, including the Netherlands
excerpt: Triennial flagship report by the European Commission and the Social Protection Committee on current and future pension adequacy across the EU, with specific attention to old-age gender inequalities built up during working lives. Women in the EU receive on average 26.1% lower pension income than men, and 5.3% of women receive no pension at all — driven by pay gaps, career interruptions and part-time work. Volume II contains country profiles for all 27 member states, including the Netherlands. The authoritative EU benchmark for the gender pension gap and the policy agenda surrounding it.

title: The impact of social and behavioural aspects on the gender pension gap in Europe
key_stats: EU gender pension gap averages 29% — more than double the 13% gender pay gap
Gap is widest in countries with high female part-time employment
Behavioural levers (defaults, communication, product design) are ones institutions can act on themselves
excerpt: Thematic article by EIOPA experts Marie Scholer and Lucian Patulea, published as an annex to the June 2024 Financial Stability Report. It documents that the EU gender pension gap averages 29% — more than double the 13% gender pay gap — and shows the gap is widest in countries with high female part-time employment. Beyond labour-market causes, differences in investment behaviour and risk appetite further widen retirement outcomes. Useful for providers because it isolates the behavioural levers — defaults, communication, product design — that financial institutions themselves can act on.

title: Towards Improved Retirement Savings Outcomes for Women
key_stats: Across the OECD, women aged 65+ receive on average 26% less retirement income than men
Gap ranges from 3% (Estonia) to 47% (Japan)
NL has the largest gap in voluntary personal pension ownership: 19% of women vs 28% of men
excerpt: OECD flagship study on how funded and private retirement savings arrangements contribute to the gender pension gap. Across the OECD, women aged 65+ receive on average 26% less retirement income than men, with gaps ranging from 3% in Estonia to 47% in Japan. The Netherlands records the largest gap in voluntary personal pension ownership: 19% of women hold a personal plan versus 28% of men. The report analyses behavioural and cultural drivers and gives concrete design options — contribution flexibility, defaults, spousal and care-period contributions — that pension providers can apply directly to product and communication design.

title: Women as the next wave of growth in US wealth management
key_stats: Women already control about a third of US household financial assets — over $10 trillion
By 2030 women are expected to control much of the $30 trillion held by baby boomers
70% of widows switch financial institution within a year of their spouse's death
Winning younger women can drive up to four times faster revenue growth
excerpt: McKinsey survey of more than 10,000 affluent US investors, nearly 3,000 of them female financial decision makers. Women already control about a third of US household financial assets — over $10 trillion — and by 2030 are expected to control much of the $30 trillion held by baby boomers. 70% of widows switch financial institution within a year of their spouse's death; retaining baby-boomer women yields roughly one-third higher revenue potential, and winning younger women can drive up to four times faster revenue growth. The standard reference for the commercial case for serving women in wealth management.

title: Managing the Next Decade of Women's Wealth
key_stats: Women control 32% of the world's wealth and add about $5 trillion to the global wealth pool every year
Women's assets were projected to reach $93 trillion by 2023
64% of women say their bank or wealth manager needs a better value proposition
30% report their relationship manager speaks to them differently because of their gender
excerpt: Global BCG study (investor survey of 300 affluent and high-net-worth individuals plus industry interviews) on women's wealth. Women control 32% of the world's wealth and add about $5 trillion to the global wealth pool every year; their assets were projected to reach $93 trillion by 2023, outpacing overall market growth. Yet 64% of women say their bank or wealth manager needs a better value proposition, and 30% report that their relationship manager speaks to them differently because of their gender. Argues for personalised, goal-based advice instead of superficial 'products for women' — directly applicable to proposition design.

title: Women in Financial Services 2020
key_stats: Financial firms forgo a $700 billion annual revenue opportunity by failing to serve women customers well
20% women on executive committees, 23% on boards
Frames gender balance as core business strategy, not an HR initiative
excerpt: Oliver Wyman's flagship study of women in financial services, covering customers, workforce and leadership. Its headline finding: financial firms forgo a $700 billion annual revenue opportunity by failing to serve women customers well across savings, investing, insurance and credit. On the supply side, progress on representation remains slow — 20% women on executive committees and 23% on boards. The report frames gender balance as core business strategy rather than an HR initiative, making it a useful boardroom anchor for any institution building a women-centred proposition.

title: The Global Findex Database 2021: headline findings on account ownership
key_stats: Account ownership reached 76% of adults worldwide, up 50% over the preceding decade
Gender gap in account ownership across developing economies fell from 9 to 6 percentage points
Women remain less likely than men to own and actively use accounts
excerpt: Headline findings on account ownership from the World Bank's Global Findex 2021, the canonical global dataset on financial inclusion. Account ownership reached 76% of adults worldwide, up 50% over the preceding decade. The gender gap in account ownership across developing economies fell from 9 to 6 percentage points — meaningful progress, but women remain less likely than men to own and actively use accounts. The reference source for quantifying the gender gap in basic banking access and for benchmarking inclusion initiatives.

title: SHEforSHIELD: Insure Women to Better Protect All
key_stats: Insurance industry could earn up to $1.7 trillion in premiums from women by 2030
About half of that premium growth comes from emerging economies
First study to size the women's insurance market as a distinct segment
excerpt: Joint study by IFC, AXA and Accenture that first sized the women's insurance market. It estimates the industry could earn up to $1.7 trillion in premiums from women by 2030, with about half coming from emerging economies, driven by women's rising incomes and strong unmet demand for protection products tailored to their lives and businesses. Based on interviews with insurers, distributors, regulators and customers combined with economic modelling. The founding reference for treating women as a distinct, commercially attractive insurance segment rather than a CSR theme.
